abstract: In nature, non-heme iron enzymes utilize dioxygen to generate high-spin iron(IV)-oxo intermediates capable of performing a wide array of challenging oxygenation reactions. While synthetic chemists have spent decades attempting to replicate this reactivity, achieving enzyme-like activation of O₂ to form high-spin iron(IV)=O species has remained a significant challenge. Here, we describe a metal–organic framework containing iron(II) sites that closely mimic the local coordination environment found in α-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenases. Upon exposure to O₂ at low temperatures, the framework facilitates the formation of high-spin iron(IV)=O species, which we characterized through a combination of in situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform (DRIFTS), variable-field Mössbauer, Fe Kβ X-ray emission, and nuclear resonance vibrational spectroscopies. We demonstrate that this framework is capable of the catalytic oxygenation of cyclohexane and the stoichiometric conversion of ethane to ethanol using O₂ as the terminal oxidant.
